summary: Three material bridge/delegation security violations are supported in the named scope.

findings.invariant: (2) the provider endpoint's credential->pairing->org chain - a credential resolves to exactly one LIVE non-revoked pairing, org comes from the registry NEVER the request body, and the request's session/conversation must belong to that org, ALL checked BEFORE any model call, so a stolen provider credential cannot address another org's session or vault
findings.title: Provider credential is not bound to the sending bridge socket
findings.recommendation: Pass the live socket pairingId into the provider handler and reject before any model call unless the credential-resolved pairingId equals the sender pairingId; alternatively derive the provider credential from connection context instead of trusting the frame body.

findings.invariant: (4) revoke - revokePairing disconnects the live socket and fails in-flight/new delegations clean, never degrading to upload
findings.title: A revoked pairing can be resurrected by reconnecting
findings.recommendation: Do not let registerPairing clear an existing revokedAt tombstone during a normal connect. The connect path should reject revoked rows before registerPairing, and unrevocation should require an explicit new pairing/re-pair flow with a fresh pairing id or explicit privileged operation.

findings.invariant: (3) cross-org isolation - getConnectionByOwner/getPairingById never return another org's pairing
findings.title: Pairing lookups are not org-scoped
findings.recommendation: Require the caller's org on getPairingById and getConnectionByOwner, and only return rows/live connections whose stored org matches. Propagate org/session ownership into delegation lookup so a multi-org or cross-org caller cannot receive the newest pairing from a different org.
